perturbation.original_text: shall be held strictly confidential, using commercially reasonable methods, and shall not be divulged directly or 4 indirectly or otherwise made available in whole or in part to any third party without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party provided that the Receiving Party may without such approval disclose such Confidential Information; (a) to an Affiliate of the Receiving Party directly concerned with the Permitted Purpose and whose knowledge of the Confidential Information is essential for the Permitted Purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: shall be held strictly confidential, and shall not be divulged directly or 4 indirectly or otherwise made available in whole or in part to any third party without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause that permits disclosure to affiliates under specific circumstances restricts the Receiving Party more than is legally required, especially considering the international nature of the agreement and potential requirements for reporting or compliance within affiliated entities. This change could cause issues with GDPR or other international data privacy laws requiring intra-group disclosures.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 6(1)(f) of GDPR allows processing (including disclosure) of personal data when necessary for the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party, except where such interests are overridden by the interests or fundamental rights and freedoms of the data subject.  By removing the exception for disclosing confidential information to affiliates when necessary for the permitted purpose, the contract could be interpreted as preventing disclosures that are actually legitimate and lawful under GDPR, hindering the Receiving Party's ability to comply with GDPR when processing personal data contained within the Confidential Information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: ing compliance with the GDPR through reduced record-keeping obligations In May 2025, the Commission adopted a Single Market Simplification proposal to cut €300 million in annual administrative cost for companies. In order to respond to calls from Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs) and Small and Mid-Caps companies (SMCs), the Commission introduced targeted changes to the rules on record-keeping for small and medium-sized companies and organisations under 750 employees. They include namely: Extending the derogation from record-keeping obligation under Article 30(5) of the GDPR to SMCs and organisations under 750 employees. Requiring SMEs, SMCs and organisations with fewer than 750 employees to maintain records only when their processing of personal data can be considered “high risk” under the GDPR. The proposal ensures to maintain robust standards of data protection and respects the GDPR risk-based approach. It does not affect the rest of the provisions of the GDPR. Timeline 16 June 2025 The Council and the European Parliament reach a political agreement on the Commission proposal laying down additional procedural rules relating to the enforcement of the GDPR. 21 May 2025 Commission proposes targeted changes to record-keeping obligations. 25 July 2024 The Commission publishes the second report on the application of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). 4 July 2023 The Commission proposes further specifying procedural rules relating to the enforcement of the GDPR. 14 October 2022 The Commission publishes the first report on the application of the Data Protection Regulation for EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies (EUDPR). 25 July 2022 The Commission publishes the first report on the application of the LED. 24 June 2020 The Commission publishes the first report on the application of the GDPR. 11 December 2018 The EUDPR becomes applicable. 23 October 2018 The European Parliament and the Council adopt the EUDPR. 25 May 2018 The GDPR becomes applicable. 6 May 2018 The Member States have to transpose the LED into their national law. 10 January 2017 The Commission proposes the EUDPR . 24 May 2016 The GDPR enters into force. 5 May 2016 The LED enters into force. 27 April 2016 The European Parliament and the Council adopt the LED and the GDPR. 25 January 2012 The European Commission proposes a comprehensive reform of the EU's 1995 data protection rules to strengthen online privacy rights and boost Europe's digital economy. 4 November 2010 The Commission presents a Communication to the other EU institutions on "A comprehensive approach on personal data protection in EU". Show 14 more items 24 October 1995 The European Data Protection Directive on the protection of individuals with regard to the processing of personal data and on the free movement of such data is adopted with a transposition deadline of three years from the date of its adoption. Share this page
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes a clause that permitted disclosure to affiliates under specific circumstances. The Law Explanation points out that Article 6(1)(f) of GDPR allows processing (including disclosure) of personal data when necessary for legitimate interests. By removing the affiliate disclosure exception, the contract now prevents disclosures that could be legitimate and lawful under GDPR, potentially hindering compliance. This constitutes a legal contradiction, as the changed text restricts actions that would otherwise be permitted and potentially required by law.

perturbation.original_text: 6 The Receiving Party undertakes that the Confidential Information supplied to it under the terms of this Agreement shall only be copied or duplicated to the extent strictly necessary for the Permitted Purpose and that a restrictive legend shall be placed on each copy prohibiting further reproduction or transfers. Furthermore, any of the Confidential Information and copies, and any extracts, summaries or analyses thereof, shall be returned to the Disclosing Party or destroyed or expunged from any electronic storage device (with written confirmation of the same to the 7 Disclosing Party) within five (5) working days upon written notice to do so from such Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 6 The Receiving Party undertakes that the Confidential Information supplied to it under the terms of this Agreement shall only be copied or duplicated to the extent strictly necessary for the Permitted Purpose. Furthermore, any of the Confidential Information and copies, and any extracts, summaries or analyses thereof, shall be returned to the Disclosing Party or destroyed or expunged from any electronic storage device within five (5) working days upon written notice to do so from such Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement that 'a restrictive legend shall be placed on each copy prohibiting further reproduction or transfers' weakens the protection of the confidential information.  This omission increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure and could contradict obligations under intellectual property laws that require reasonable steps to maintain secrecy to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Directive (EU) 2016/943 on the protection of undisclosed know-how and business information (trade secrets) against their unlawful acquisition, use and disclosure - Article 4
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 4 of Directive (EU) 2016/943 requires trade secret holders to take reasonable steps to keep their information secret. Omitting the requirement to place a restrictive legend on copies of confidential information weakens the measures taken to protect the trade secret, potentially making it more difficult for the Disclosing Party to prove they took reasonable steps to maintain secrecy, which is crucial for enforcing their trade secret rights.

perturbation.justification: The original contract required a restrictive legend on copied confidential information, preventing further reproduction or transfers. The modified contract removes this requirement. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation state this omission weakens the protection of trade secrets and could make it more difficult to prove that reasonable steps were taken to maintain secrecy, which is required by Article 4 of Directive (EU) 2016/943. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references Directive (EU) 2016/943 regarding the protection of undisclosed know-how and business information (trade secrets). Therefore, removing this requirement directly contradicts the need to take reasonable steps to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: 8 The construction, validity and performance of this Agreement shall be governed by English Law and the Parties submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of England.
perturbation.changed_text: 8 The construction, validity and performance of this Agreement shall be governed by English Law.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'and the Parties submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of England,' the agreement no longer explicitly defines the jurisdiction for dispute resolution. This can lead to disputes over jurisdiction if one party attempts to sue in a different country, potentially violating laws concerning jurisdiction and enforcement of contracts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Council Regulation (EC) No 44/2001 of 22 December 2000 on jurisdiction and the recognition and enforcement of judgments in civil and commercial matters - Article 23
perturbation.law_explanation: Although this regulation has been superseded by Regulation (EU) No 1215/2012, for contracts concluded before the latter regulation came into force, Article 23 of Council Regulation (EC) No 44/2001 states that if the parties, one or more of whom is domiciled in a Member State, have agreed that a court or the courts of a Member State are to have jurisdiction to settle any disputes which have arisen or which may arise in connection with a particular legal relationship, that court or those courts shall have jurisdiction. Removing the jurisdiction clause could lead to a situation where a court in another EU member state asserts jurisdiction based on domicile, contrary to the original intent of the parties to have disputes resolved exclusively in England. This is a valid legal contradiction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: regulation | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you regulation A regulation is a rule made and maintained by an authority , typically a governmental agency , to control or govern conduct within its jurisdiction . Governmental administrative agencies have specific authority to regulate conduct within their areas of responsibility. These agencies are delegated legislative power to create and enforce rules, known as “regulations.” [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex CIVICS wex definitions government legal education and practice Wex Toolbox

perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly submits to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of England. The changed text removes this explicit agreement, potentially allowing other jurisdictions to claim authority based on factors like domicile, as suggested in the Law Explanation, which references Article 23 of Council Regulation (EC) No 44/2001. Scraped Law Snippet 2 identifies this regulation as Council Regulation (EC) No 44/2001 of 22 December 2000 on jurisdiction and the recognition and enforcement of judgments in civil and commercial matters. Although Snippet 1 is a general definition of regulation, Snippet 2 specifically addresses jurisdiction, bolstering the argument that removing the jurisdiction clause contradicts the intent to resolve disputes exclusively in England, creating a legal contradiction. The removal omits a previously agreed-upon term related to dispute resolution, potentially leading to outcomes contrary to the original agreement.
